Sort the array and use two left-to-right pointers (i=0, j=1) to track the difference, using inner while loops to aggressively skip identical values after every match to guarantee only distinct pairs are counted.

Recursively explore adding valid opening and closing parentheses to a single shared StringBuilder, ensuring you delete the last appended character after every recursive return to cleanly backtrack and try the alternative branch.

Recursively build subsets by making a "take it" choice (add the element, explore, then erase it) and a "leave it" choice (just explore) at each index, ensuring you save a deep copy of the shared list when you reach the end of the array.

Recursively build permutations by looping through the original array, using a boolean "visited" array to skip already-used numbers, and applying the "add, recurse, remove" pattern to explore all sequences.

Recursively branch the 1-step choice before the 2-step choice to guarantee lexicographical order, making sure to undo the choice after exploring and saving a deep copy of the path when the remaining steps hit zero

Recursively traverse the grid by exploring the Down ('D') branch before the Right ('R') branch to guarantee lexicographical order, using string concatenation to implicitly manage the backtracking state until reaching the bottom-right corner

cue: Two Heaps | Max Heap + Min Heap
oneLiner: Maintain a Max Heap for the smaller half of the stream and a Min Heap for the larger half, rebalancing immediately if their size difference exceeds 1. To find the median, return the top of the larger heap. If sizes are equal, return the top of the Max Heap (Do not take the average!)

Bundle the start and end times into a custom object and sort the array in ascending order strictly by their end times. Iterate through the sorted list, incrementing your count and updating your lastEndTime tracker only when a job's start time is greater than or equal to the previous job's end time

To merge K sorted linked lists, push the head of each list into a Min Heap using a custom value comparator. Pop the smallest node, attach it to your dummy tail, and if poppedNode.next exists, push it into the heap

To find the A-th largest element, first pad the answer array with A-1 negative ones and build an initial Min Heap using the first A elements. Then, iterate the rest of the array: if the incoming element is greater than heap.peek(), poll() the root and add the new element, appending heap.peek() to your answer array at every step

To find all anti-diagonals, trace from two sets of starting points: the top row (col 0 to N-1) and the rightmost column (row 1 to N-1). From each starting point (r, c), collect elements by moving down-left (r+1, c-1) and place them into the current row of the output matrix. Java's default initialization handles the trailing zeros automatically

To set matrix zeroes in O(1) space, use Row 0 and Col 0 as flag arrays. First, check if Row 0 or Col 0 inherently contain zeros and store this in two booleans. Next, iterate from i=1, j=1; if A[i][j]==0, set A[i][0]=0 and A[0][j]=0. Iterate from 1,1 again and apply the zeros based on the flags. Finally, update Row 0 and Col 0 based on the saved booleans

To check if two trees are identical, do not flatten them into arrays. Use simultaneous recursion: if both nodes are null, return 1; if only one is null or values differ, return 0. Recursively check if isSameTree(A.left, B.left) AND isSameTree(A.right, B.right) both return 1

Use a HashMap to store the last_seen_index of each element. Execution: Iterate through the array. If the current element exists in the map, calculate the distance: current_index - map.get(element), and update the global minimum distance. Regardless of whether it was found or not, always update the map with the current_index so future matches use the closest possible index. If the global minimum is never updated, return -1

Extract all start times into one array and end times into another. Sort both arrays. Use two pointers (i for start, j for end). If start[i] < end[j], a meeting is starting, increment current rooms and move i. Else, a meeting is ending, decrement current rooms and move j. Keep tracking the global maximum

Use an int[128] for ASCII safety. Count frequencies required by T. Expand window by moving e. If a collected character helps meet T's requirement, increment formed. while (formed == required), record the minimum length, decrement the frequency of character at s, and move s forward. If you drop a required character, decrement formed to break the shrink loop and go back to expanding.

Execution: > 1. The Core Relation: To reach step N, you either came from step N-1 or N-2. Therefore: ways(N) = (ways(N-1) + ways(N-2)) % 1000000007. 2. Top-Down (Memoization): Init array with -1. Base cases: if (N == 0 || N == 1) return 1;. Check cache: if (dp[N] != -1) return dp[N];. Recurse, modulo, and save. (Time: O(N), Space: O(N)). 3. Bottom-Up (Tabulation): Base cases dp[0]=1, dp[1]=1. Loop i = 2 to N: dp[i] = (dp[i-1] + dp[i-2]) % 1000000007. 4. Senior Optimization (Slider): We only need the last two steps. Use a=1, b=1. Loop from 2 to N: c = (a + b) % 1000000007, then shift a = b and b = c. Return c. (Time: O(N), Space: O(1))

Fix the middle element j, then count all strictly smaller elements to its left (left_smaller) and strictly greater elements to its right (right_larger). For each $j$, the number of valid triplets is simply left_smaller * right_larger, bringing the O(N^3) brute force down to an optimal O(N^2) time complexity

Convert the integer to a string and use an $O(N^2)$ nested loop to generate all contiguous subarrays, maintaining a running product inside the inner loop. Check each running product against a HashSet, returning 0 immediately if a duplicate is found, or 1 if all loops finish successfully.

The implementation is nearly identical to 0/1 Knapsack, but when you pick an item, you stay on the current row i instead of moving back to i-1 so the same item can be selected again. Calculate dp[i][j] as the maximum of either skipping the item completely (dp[i-1][j]) or taking it if capacity allows (value[i] + dp[i][j - weight[i]])

Treat the outer loop i as introducing a "new item" and the inner loop j as testing every possible "bag size," where the array index always represents the capacity. Read the transition equation as a physical choice for the current bag size j: you either skip the item and keep yesterday's best profit (prev[j]), or pack it by adding its value to yesterday's best profit for the leftover space (prev[j - weight])

Use two boolean arrays: visited (global memory to avoid redundant work) and pathVisited (active path memory to detect cycles). When entering a node, mark both as true; if you ever encounter a neighbor where pathVisited is already true, you have found a cycle! When backtracking out of a node, set pathVisited to false (picking up your breadcrumb), but leave visited as true to ensure you never process that node again.

Convert the Edge List into an Adjacency List first. Use a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) to greedily pick the minimum weight edge that connects an unvisited node to the growing tree. Always maintain a visited array to prevent cycles, and ensure the Priority Queue is initialized with a custom comparator for the weights

Convert the Edge List into an Adjacency List first. Use a Min-Heap (Priority Queue) to greedily pick the minimum weight edge that connects an unvisited node to the growing tree. Always maintain a visited array to prevent cycles, and ensure the Priority Queue is initialized with a custom comparator for the weights

To handle weight-2 edges, insert a dummy node in the middle of each to convert them into two weight-1 edges. Then, run a standard BFS from the source and return the distance the moment you reach the destination.
It is simple cycle detection using DFS, if cycle is present , then not possible or else it is possible

Calculate the in-degree for all nodes and push those with an in-degree of 0 into a Queue (use a PriorityQueue if you need the lexicographically smallest order). Pop nodes, add them to your answer array, and decrement their neighbors' in-degrees—pushing any neighbor that drops to 0 into the queue—and finally check if the answer size matches the total nodes to ensure no cycles exist

exampleProblem: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stocks II
oneLiner: Don't manually track peaks and valleys. Use a Greedy approach: loop from index 1 to N, and anytime A[i] > A[i-1], simply add A[i] - A[i-1] to your total profit. Every upward daily step contributes to the total
Don't calculate the factorial (overflow). Count the pairs of (2, 5). 2s are abundant, so just count the 5s using a loop: count += n / i where i multiplies by 5 each step.
You cannot divide under a modulo! To compute (A / B) mod M, turn division into multiplication using Fermat's formula: (A * B^{M-2})mod M

If a problem asks if ANY valid state exists (e.g., Sudoku), return boolean from your recursive calls to instantly kill the search once found. If a problem asks to generate ALL valid states (e.g., N-Queens), keep the return type void. Let the recursion naturally unfold and save the state only inside your base case

The secret to deletion is finding the "Last Safe Node" so you don't accidentally delete nodes shared by other words. As you traverse down the target word, constantly update a temp pointer and reference character (rc) whenever the current node has more than 1 child or its eow is true (indicating an overlap). Once you reach the end of the target word, immediately set its eow to false. Finally, if that last node has 0 children, go back to your temp node and sever the dead branch by setting temp.children[rc - 'a'] = null

To find the shortest unique prefix, upgrade your standard Trie node to include an int freq counter. During the insertion phase, increment this frequency for every single node you step on to track exactly how many words share that specific path. Then, to find the unique prefix for a word, traverse the Trie again and stop the exact moment you land on a node where curr.freq == 1. The substring from the root down to that specific node is mathematically guaranteed to be your shortest unique prefix.

the core engine is treating integers as 32-bit paths (MSB to LSB) in an empty Bitwise Trie, allowing you to make the "Greedy Choice" by always searching for the opposite bit (1 - bit) to guarantee the highest possible value

you don't invent new logic; you capitalize on the rule that XOR is its own inverse by building a prefixXOR array and feeding it directly into your first Trie, with the vital upgrade of dropping "index anchors" at the leaf nodes. When a match is found, you pull those two indices to define your subarray boundaries (Left = min + 1, Right = max), which immediately allows you to filter the results through Scaler's strict three-tier tie-breaker: highest XOR first, then shortest length, and finally the earliest starting index.

exampleProblem: Flatten Binary Tree to Linked List
oneLiner: The Architecture: Use a recursive function returning a (head, tail) pair to flatten subtrees bottom-up.  The Wiring: Connect them by setting root.right = left_head, then left_tail.right = right_head.  The Trap: Always explicitly sever the left pointer (root.left = null) to prevent invalid tree cycles.  The Edge Case: If a node only has a left child, the true end of that list is the left_tail, not the left_head

The Core Secret (The Bridge): Odd ASCII characters are never adjacent to each other. Evens are never adjacent. If you separate them into two halves, you just need to find one valid pair (a bridge) to connect the Odd half to the Even half.The Trap (Brute Force - $O(N^2)$): Iterating through every single Odd character and comparing it against every single Even character will cause a Time Limit Exceeded (TLE) on massive strings.The HashSet Pivot ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Since there are only 26 letters, dump characters into two HashSets (Odds and Evens). This reduces the nested loop to a maximum of $13 \times 13 = 169$ constant-time checks.The Masterpiece ($O(N)$ Time, $O(1)$ Space): Ditch the sets. Traverse the string once to find four extremes: minOdd, maxOdd, minEven, and maxEven. Test the 4 cross-combinations. If the absolute difference of any pair is != 1, return true. Otherwise, return false.

The Architecture (Fixed Size): Because any valid permutation of A must be exactly the length of A, the window size never changes. You do not need a while loop to shrink it.The Initial Setup: Create freq_A and freq_B arrays (size 26). Fully populate freq_A, but only populate freq_B with the first A.length() characters of string B. Check if they match using Arrays.equals(); if yes, count++.The Slide ($O(N)$): Loop from i = A.length() to the end of B. Slide the rigid frame by doing exactly two operations:Add Right: Increment the frequency of the new character entering the window: B.charAt(i).Drop Left: Decrement the frequency of the old character falling out of the window: B.charAt(i - A.length()).The Array Index Trap: When dropping the left character, do not accidentally use the raw index math (i - A.length()) inside the frequency array. You must fetch the actual character from string B first, then subtract 'a' to get the correct frequency bucket.

That base case bounds swap is the final boss of Edit Distance. Now that you have navigated the 1-indexed string shift and the unique Row 0 / Col 0 initializations, this architecture is fully yours.  Here is your 4-step SRS cheat sheet for the Edit Distance problem. Drop this directly into your notes:  The "Edit Distance Tabulation" SRS Cheat Sheet The State Definition: dp[i][j] represents the minimum number of operations required to convert the first i characters of String A into the first j characters of String B.  The Base Case Trap: Size your table as new int[N+1][M+1]. Unlike LCS, Row 0 and Column 0 cannot be 0.  Row 0: Converting an empty string to String B takes j inserts (dp[0][j] = j).  Col 0: Converting String A to an empty string takes i deletes (dp[i][0] = i).  The Match Transition: Shift your string indices back by one (A.charAt(i-1) == B.charAt(j-1)). If the characters match, no operation is needed. Inherit the cost from the diagonal: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]  The Mismatch Transition: If the characters do not match, you must perform exactly 1 operation. Take the minimum cost of the three possible paths and add 1:  Insert: Look Left (dp[i][j-1])  Delete: Look Up (dp[i-1][j])  Replace: Look Diagonal (dp[i-1][j-1])  dp[i][j] = 1 + Math.min(Replace, Math.min(Insert, Delete))

The Object Mapping: You cannot separate heights and widths. Always map the 2D array into a custom Pair or Envelope object array so they travel together during sorting.  The Timeline Sort: To reduce the 2D problem into a 1D LIS problem, sort the array by one of the dimensions (e.g., Height ascending). This guarantees that any envelope j before envelope i is at least short enough to potentially fit inside i.  The Strict 2D LIS Check: Run the exact LIS nested loops, but your inner if condition must strictly check both dimensions. Equal sizes do not fit inside each other! if (env[j].w < env[i].w && env[j].h < env[i].h)  The Global Max: Just like standard LIS, the biggest nested doll doesn't necessarily end with the very last envelope in the array. Track ans = Math.max(ans, dp[i])

The FAANG Trap: Do not write a 1D recursive function that uses a while loop to check if a string is a palindrome. That creates an $O(N^3)$ algorithm that will hit TLE. You must split the problem into two distinct DP phases to achieve $O(N^2)$.Phase 1: The 2D Palindrome Cache (The Lookup Table): * Use the "Gap Strategy" (diagonal traversal) to build a boolean[][] isPal table.isPal[i][j] is true if the substring from i to j is a palindrome. This gives you $O(1)$ lookups for Phase 2.Phase 2: The 1D Cuts Array (The State Definition): * cuts[i] represents the minimum number of cuts needed to perfectly partition the prefix string from index 0 to i.The Partition Transition:Base Case: If isPal[0][i] == true, the whole prefix is a palindrome. It needs 0 cuts! (cuts[i] = 0).The Cut Loop: If it's not a palindrome, try placing a cut at every index j (from 1 to i).If the right side of the cut is a palindrome (isPal[j][i] == true), then the total cuts = the cuts needed for the left side plus one new cut.cuts[i] = Math.min(cuts[i], cuts[j-1] + 1)

Create a color[] array initialized to -1. 2. Loop through all nodes (to handle disconnected components). If unvisited, paint it 0 and start DFS. 3. The Core Trick: In DFS, paint unvisited neighbors with the opposite color using 1 - col[src]. 4. If a neighbor is already visited and has the same color as the current node (col[nbr] == col[src]), return false!
Cycle Graph Chromatic Number: Even nodes = perfect alternating bipartite (2 colors). Odd nodes = collision at the end (3 colors). return 2 + (A % 2);
